item.title: decent in theory, but wording could be clearer.
item.content:encoded: I like it, but I was a little confused as to who was being threatened the first time I read it. The only CLEAR indication that the caller is being threatened is that obviously, the other party can't control their vehicle. Then again, maybe this was partly because I initially misinterpreted the nature of the object?

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Noting that the images could not be properly sourced, and are assumed to be non-compliant. As such, both have been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fridge horror hits when you start to wonder "Does the intended recipient hear what the caller is saying?" Because if that's the case then are they pleading with the caller, wondering what they're talking about and what do they mean someone has a gun to my head, what's happening-OH GOD

And if that's the case, does that audio get recorded too? And did FD get a rise out of it, in addition to his car-crash thing?
I'm gonna have to go with Gears' Razor and assume, "Yes" to all of the above.
This is brilliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I hate this skip so much, because it is so cruel. In an effort to save their loved ones, these victims cause them infinite anguish by their own deaths—and the deaths of everyone else they crash into!
It also makes me hate the Foundation. Nevermind the evil of this object, it must be contained, not destroyed.
+1

THAT SAID, I definitely agree with previous comments that there is a lot of untapped potential with regards to research and testing. We want to know what happens when a researcher denies the orders of the caller—and I bet the Foundation would too!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is the first thing I thought.
What happens if they interact with the voice in a way that's not anticipated?
Like by not committing suicide.
Or doing the Liam Neeson speech from Taken.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What was in the negatives when I found it has now grown so much. Good to see this one not fail!

Upvote, if I haven't already.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think an interview log would be nice. I mean, it wouldn't be much of a bother to hook the SCP up, get a researcher to drive around until the phone call arrives, and then question the male voice. That's just my 2 cents, though.

I really like this, though! A confident upvote for you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What if the caller were to dismiss the hostage situation as a prank and do nothing? I would like it if the "voice" had a plan b, like for instance telling the caller something more sinister or sending him an image with the loved one tied up, beaten up, missing fingers or something like that. Other than that +1 very nice concept!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not an actual hostage situation. It's fake audio designed to make people hurt themselves.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Editing issues aside, has it ever been able to follow through on a threat? The fact that this issue wasn't even mentioned tips my no-vote to downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love that this was just set up so some nutbag could get his yayas.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The concept/hook is too contrived for me to take seriously.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Testing must take place on a base transceiver station (BTS) mounted on a cell tower in Site 76, in a 5 m by 5 m by 35 m converted elevator shaft which must be maintained at Foundation B-Standard Faraday Integrity or higher at all times.

What could they possibly test while it was in a Faraday cage like that? To test it, wouldn't they have to put it in a place where no civilians would come within range of the antenna, so that they could drive cars around near it?

In the event of a containment breach …

Since the article no longer uses a computer virus, shouldn't this part be removed?

SCP-1558 is an anomalous cellular network antenna, designed for compatibility with the ████ network standard.

Wouldn't what cellular network an antenna works with depend on the hardware and software which uses that antenna?

SCP-1558 has the capability of ‘intercepting’ telephone calls placed through transceiver stations it infects and covertly replacing the audio of said calls. A

I think that "infects" is one you missed taking out.

The call must be placed by an individual in a vehicle traveling 65 km/h or greater.

Is there any reason why it doesn't affect calls when the person with the cellular phone in the vehicle is the recipient rather than the caller?

SCP-1558’s substituted audio exhibits similar characteristics in every recorded instance: A two-person interaction involving a deep male voice (SCP-1558-1) and a perfect replica of the voice of the intended recipient of the call. The substituted audio demonstrates an awareness of the caller’s circumstances and relationship with the intended recipient.

Does the recipient hear the altered call? If so, do they hear all of it or just what the caller is saying?

Upon the completion of a SCP-1558 intercepted call, a recorded audio file of said call is downloaded to SCP-1558-2’s hard drive at the next time SCP-1558-2 has internet access and power.

Does the cellular network antenna imitate a Internet capable cellular phone so that it can upload the information to the SCP-1558-2? Or does the antenna connect to the Internet is some way the Foundation can't determine, and when SCP-1558-2 connects to the Internet it downloads the information from a source the Foundation can't track?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad that I didn't see the virus version of this. Especially since my headcannon for this is that it's a sentient, malicious entity that hijacks the calls, and that F D was trying to find him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it other than the second picture. That's a network stack, router, a switch, and what looks like a firewall. I see no server blades, so there would be no storage. I see nothing that is a storage device in any way. That's it. That's all the bugs me.

Ps, that bottom thing is power. Maybe a back up power supply.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not a fan of this one per se, but it instilled the same profound horror in me that J.G. Ballard's Crash had, so I have to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I upvoted this thing because I really like the effect, even though I was thinking "Viruses (and programs in general) don't work like that."

What you have here is a supernatural entity that for some reason likes to possess machines, specifically cell towers, instead of people. Work with it that way and you will get all the Comp. Sci. folks off your back over the bullshit science.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Computers are magic! (Spoiler: They're not)

The problem with trying to involve modern technology with magical SCP effects normally associated with non-electronics is that electronics are highly precise and well-defined and do not translate well between the mediums of bits and bytes to physical objects.

If it's a special (note: not magical) computer made with future technology that we cannot reliably take apart and analyze because we're afraid of breaking it, fine, I could maybe live with that (MAYBE).
If it's an IBM Model M that reliably “downloads your mental state to a regular hard drive whose data Foundation scientists cannot copy or read” if you engage in stream of consciousness typing, that's where the trout comes out.
If it's a “computer virus” that spreads itself like an independent organism, you're gonna get slapped with said trout for a while unless you did a /really/ good job (e.g. no independent programs a la Megaman.EXE).

We know how computers work because we designed them that way (and other stuff like math and numbers), so if you really insist on making a electronics SCP, either you should do some research on how things actually work down to the nitty-gritty details (up to a reasonable point - e.g. no instantaneous bullshit response) or try to stay away from a relation to the technology like how Portkeys in Harry Potter are just random objects that just happened to teleport you. Otherwise, like 033, you'd end up with a SCP that basically detonates the universe in an instant the moment a troll researcher wonders what will happen if he does ${FOO} and that'll just evoke an angry response as opposed to “zomg creepy”.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've removed the 'magicy' element and made it one anomalous antenna. I think this improves the article a lot, thanks so much for your input, Syka and Anaxagoras!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, with sufficient magic, that could work, although I agree that in this case, you want to use as little magic as possible. Seriously, just drop the viral aspect. The scenario itself is plenty horrifying without it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There is not one kind of "cell tower". There are a myriad of different systems - "GSM" can colloquially refer to HSPA and WCDMA sorts of networks, but it's more correctly used to describe 2G EDGE-style networks. Additionally, there's several manufacturers of equipment of this - and that ranges from dumber kit from the early 00s to the higher tech stuff in the new towers - a virus can't just 'hack' random ones and transfer itself - since not every tower will have the same kit powering it. Unless the virus is made by a team of experts that have kit on hand and have vulnerabilities for each… not going to happen.

Additionally - "Upon the completion of a SCP-1558 intercepted call, a recorded audio file of said call is downloaded to SCP-1558-2’s hard drive, regardless of Internet connection or power status." This here is enough for a downvote. If it can't contact the tower via the internet, it still works. If it's off, it still works. Because somehow this radio tower virus can use the powers of magic to arrange magnetic bits on a powered off hard drive to form the data that makes up an encoded sound file, then via the same MoIP (Magic over IP) connection, update the file allocation table of the HDD so that the file 'exists' to the OS. Note that files aren't stored sequentially - they are quite commonly fragmented (see something I made yonks ago that explains it simply here). So to work around that, you would need to have the contents of the HDD right there to read off and work - which is just impossible, as that's just "it happens because it happens and it's *spooky* oooh, disregarding the fact that this isn't how servers or viruses work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why not just have it be a program installed on one cell tower? That probably makes it safe class, but removes the bad science.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The picture makes it for me. It's neat until I realize how many of those towers I see every day, and the idea that something's in there tickles my fancy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay. If somebody knows more about cell systems than me, and can make the language technically correct, please change it. Because I don't really know what you mean, Syka.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"SCP-1558 is an anomalous computer virus program which infects GSM standard cellular network base transceiver stations."

GSM standard cell network towers, eh? Made by who? Motorola? Toshiba? Fujitsu? Not to mention GSM is GRPS and EDGE, both of which are rarely used - you would be looking at the 3G networks HSPA and advanced WCDMA - not the 2G GSM, which is only a fallback which near 0 modern phones would use, unless 3/4G was down.

Changing it from 'virus' to 'program' is making it worse - since a virus IS a program, and a malicious program could be deemed a virus.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm with you on this one.

One copy of a virus is not how virus' work. Anomalous only gets you so far =/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Patched up. Thanks for the help.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is decently well written and a neat concept…

…were it not for the glaring fact that viruses do not work that way. If it moves between cell towers, then it's a "program capable of relocating itself" between such nodes. If it's hosted on a server, as you suggest, then it's a "program capable of taking control" of such towers. Either way, you have not described a computer virus. Downvoting due to bad science until this is fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, who do you think writes viruses? Usually just some guy/gal with a destructive attitude or a grudge.

But in any case, my interpretation is that this was his ghost. He did commit suicide, after all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A tad different. Stretches my disbelief a little by the fact that some random guy just came up with a virus like this, but I like it overall.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's the first one in a minute that's actually made me feel a little twingy in the head, the brief buzzing sensation that something in the world has just gone horribly wrong. Very succinct and to the point, giving exactly what needed giving and no more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An anomaly that convinces people to make traffic accidents by pretending to threaten their loved ones appeals to me, and the rest of this article does a good job of supporting that idea. I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's a good idea. Changed accordingly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The intended recipient of the call must be an individual the caller considers a “loved one.”

Wording seems a bit odd. "of close relation to the caller" or "has a personal relationship to the caller" might create better tone, but I'd ask around for an even better wording. As is, it feels out of place.

As of now, no vote. Not sure if my late-night mental state allows me fair judgement.
